data.title: L3Harris Enters The Arsenal Of Freedom, But It's Overvalued (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
The Department of Defense has proposed a $1B convertible preferred investment into L3Harris (LHX) Missile Solutions, offering a revolutionary shift in the finance of defense production. It cannot be stated enough how important defense modernization is during a cold war with America's Eastern communist counterparts, and Russia, let alone the smaller threats that challenge free-market capitalism and the rule of law. This $1B LHX deal is a clear prelude to Trump's recently proposed $1.5T defense budget for 2027. Interestingly, this L3Harris deal shows the state explicitly absorbing capex risk to ensure throughput, sustainment, and replenishment—all at speed. This makes L3Harris and other defense stocks appealing, not primarily due to revenue drivers (though that's bullish, too), but because of lower volatility and longer duration. This leads to a reduced risk profile for defense cash flows, which is appealing—and necessary macroeconomically—during a late-cycle capex boom in terms of equity market valuations. Since my last LHX analysis in December 2024, in which I issued a Buy rating, the stock has gained 53% in price.

Industrial Bottleneck & Capital Structure

Propulsion throughput has become the binding constraint in modern missile production, as if propulsion stalls, the entire production chain stalls. Solid rocket motor capacity is capex-intensive and requires multi-year investments with constrained alternative use. Once shortages become exposed, propulsion becomes politically non-optional and price signals become more irrelevant, and so the state has an incentive to move from a buyer to a capital partner to accelerate geopolitical security.

This explains why the DoD proposed its $1B convertible preferred investment into L3Harris Missile Solutions. It's designed to fund capacity expansion to tackle the bottleneck and then convert at IPO—L3Harris retains control, but the state is an anchor investor, contributing to American equity market stability and geopolitical security at the same time.

LHX shares surged 11% in the pre-market on the day I wrote this article, which makes sense given that the government's equity-like capital beneath the parent company removed the burden of incremental debt or the utilization of internal cash—balance sheet stress was, in effect, reduced probabilistically. This has lowered the company's weighted average cost of capital by reducing downside risk and raising the future return on invested capital. A compressed discount rate is the winning card here, even though higher near-term revenue was not part of the structural re-rate.

Is This Part of a Capex Bubble?

There is a critical risk in the current capex boom that I believe the Trump administration may broadly be glossing over, which is policy-driven overbuild. Policymakers are optimizing for there being no single point of structural failure in the geopolitical environment, which makes absolute sense from first principles. However, mid-cycle IRR for shareholders is at risk without stated intent to pair the capital deployed in manufacturing programs with multi-year procurement framework agreements. In the case of L3Harris and the $1B DoD investment, multi-year procurement is highly likely even if not yet locked, so capex bubble risks diminish far more than with companies feeding on private-sector demand and re-shoring on tariff-evasion incentives.

I admit that there is the risk here that capacity is built for resilience and not utilization; short-range missile plants are highly irreversible investments, and even though overcapacity might be identified on a spreadsheet, it makes sense for national-security insurance. Washington might not mind equity market cyclicality if it means America is restructured for real economy resilience—but the dollar and the capital markets could suffer by negating the very primary imperative that has turned America into the world's foremost financiers.

While in the purely private markets utilization is the dictator of margins, in defense the government can pay to keep capacity alive even when organic demand would not fully load the asset. In fact, the Army Science Board ("ASB") has argued that multiyear contracts with minimum sustaining rates can achieve a twofold advantage: industry investment is incentivized while single points of failure are avoided, essentially by creating a warm base rather than chasing peak utilization. America is in "insurance mode," but it should remember the imperative is not to turn insurance into the main deal. Equity is Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.A) (BRK.B) golden goose—the insurance underwriting simply increases the compounding advantage.

Wider Utilization Analysis

L3Harris generally has strong utilization. At January 3, 2025, total backlog was $34.2B, with the company expecting about 45% of that revenue recognized by the end of FY25 and about 75% by the end of FY26.

However, the upside case for L3Harris shareholders—at least right now—is not more backlog, but lower equity risk premia. A classic bust is, bluntly, unlikely here, because we have state capital and a multi-year framework with tax-payer support that is far stronger than utilization-collapse risk from a self-funded capex overhang.

In fact, if the state's objective is resilience, it may tolerate or even prefer excess capacity. Although that can cap margin upside as the project isn't optimized for IRR, and so there is an ironic pseudo-socialization of returns downward here through heavier government intervention—even though Trump seems to speak with almost "pure" free-market allegiance.

Valuation

L3Harris is too expensive now for an entry point if you're seeking elite alpha. Valuations for American defense companies do have the potential to become further overvalued, but engaging in speculative excess has never been a wise long-term capital allocation framework.

The company's forward PEG non-GAAP ratio is 26% higher than the sector median, implying a requirement of heavy upward revisions, which is plausible but still overvalued. The stock's risk-premia re-rating was already fully priced quickly after the $1B DoD investment in LHX hit public streams.

Short-term overvaluation is indicated in simple measures like the 14-week RSI at nearly 80. I expect a minor reversion as sentiment stabilizes, though I do see consolidation as the medium-term price action from here. There is likely incremental upside to come as 2027's expanded DoD budget becomes more tangible, but I view defense holdings as relative anti-cyclical ballasts in light of a heavily cyclical AI capex regime currently exhibiting late-cycle price and valuation dynamics.

In the above chart I have outlined what I consider a realistic and worthy entry band, at which stock price my 20% or greater price target threshold would likely be met for a Buy rating. At this juncture, I am assigning a Hold rating on the back of an assumption that sentiment will not stay elevated continuously and that following a pullback, equity returns could be about 10% in 12 months from the present $340 per share. LHX does have a beta of 0.66, so this is one reason to consider owning the stock regardless of its lower alpha potential after recent highs.

Conclusion: Hold

I am an owner of defense stocks, but I only own those that are Western-aligned—or, more poetically stated, part of the "arsenal of freedom," as toured by Hegseth, or "arsenal of democracy," as made famous by Roosevelt.

At this critical juncture in history, the Western requirement for deterrence cannot be understated. The proposed $1B convertible preferred investment into L3Harris Missile Solutions is a change in the way defense capacity is financed. The state is explicitly absorbing capex and utilization risk in order to stabilize a fundamental geopolitical bottleneck.

As a result, LHX's equity risk premia has compressed, and credit durability has strengthened, even if incremental margin upside is capped over time due to durability over growth utilization imperatives for now. Even though the stock is expensive after its re-rating, it is a prudent holding in a late-cycle, phase-1 AI infrastructure environment.

data.title: Palantir: Beware The AI Rotation
data.description:
Thesis Summary

AI is not old news yet, but it’s definitely not new news.

We’ve been in the midst of an AI revolution for over two years, and in this time, the market has certainly matured.

First, it was about the chips (GPUs), then infrastructure buildout (data centers), and the next phase will be all about monetizing AI in the real world.

As such, investors should try to think about which companies will benefit most once the AI infrastructure is laid out.

Without a doubt, Palantir (PLTR) is the clearest winner of this cohort, and although the valuation is already rich, I expect strong returns in 2026 as this narrative continues to mature.

As I laid out in my last article, Palantir is uniquely positioned to benefit from what comes next, and I’ll explain exactly how below.

The “AI Cycle”

As AI evolves, so must our understanding of the AI market. To keep things simple, I’d argue that we can divide the evolution of AI into three phases.

Bear in mind, these are all interconnected, each one building on the previous one but bringing something new to the table.

Phase One: Compute

NVIDIA (NVDA) and other chipmakers were the first beneficiaries of the AI revolution. In the early days, it was all about having as many of the core production elements of AI, which were, for the most part, Nvidia GPUs.

In fact, there was not enough going around, with a clear shortage of GPUs.

This was around the time we saw Nvidia stock fly over 200%, since they seemed to hold the key to the AI kingdom.

In a sense, GPUs were the first core component of the AI value chain.

Phase Two: Infrastructure

Though chips are, of course, still very important, new competitors began to arise.

Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and even Intel (INTC) all came for a slice of the pie, and as recent evidence suggests, they have succeeded.

So, the market began to look ahead at what the next stage of AI was, and the term hyperscaler became a household name.

Value moved higher up the chain, towards those who could take the GPUs and put them together into data centers, which required another layer of complexity, as we added power into the mix and other complementary technology.

And of course, those ahead of the curve were rewarded.

Nebius (NBIS), for example, a favourite of mine, was the best-performing Software System stock of the year.

But again, profits attract competition, and now the market has been flooded by new competitors.

CoreWeave (CRWV) went public, and Bitcoin miners began to repurpose their assets towards the AI market.

Again, this is a natural evolution of markets. High profits lead to higher investment, which eventually squeezes margins.

Phase Three: Software

As we close out 2025, the data center market seems quite crowded, with valuations already pricing in high growth.

Now it’s all about who can build useful applications on top of this infrastructure.

This is similar to what happened with the internet.

Ultimately, the companies that benefited the most were those capable of building applications on the internet that offered massive value to consumers.

Alphabet’s (GOOGL) Google, Meta’s (META) Facebook, or Microsoft’s (MSFT) operating system.

These have been the best-performing stocks of the past decade and have redefined everyday life in ways most wouldn't have anticipated.

The obvious trade, betting on AI buildout, is behind us; now we must look ahead at what companies can actually put this to good use.

Palantir is Where Money is Made

If you ask me, 2026 will be the year when we move from Phase Two to Phase Three, and Palantir is uniquely positioned to benefit.

Made for Purpose-Built AI

While general-purpose AI has reached a basic level of usefulness, which I already believe is game-changing, the real barrier is applying this AI at scale, in niche environments, and in a way that can benefit corporations.

Palantir was designed to solve exactly this problem. Its AIP platform sits at the intersection of data ingestion, model orchestration, and decision workflows.

Specifically, Palantir’s ontology, which brings real-world assets into the digital world, is ideal.

The Ontology transforms your digital assets — including data, models, and processes — into a dynamic, actionable representation of the business for all users to leverage.

Source: Business Insider

As I have argued before, this is the missing piece in AI applications today. The link between the digital and real world, which bridges the gap between theoretical models towards practical applications.

Made for Enterprise-Level Deployment

It’s one thing to build AI, but it’s a whole other thing to do so in a secure and reliable environment. Size becomes an issue after a certain point, but size is also where the real money is.

Palantir’s long history with defence, intelligence, and critical infrastructure customers has forced it to operate under these constraints from day one.

That experience is now becoming a competitive advantage as commercial customers demand the same robustness.

Made to Last and Compound

Once Palantir is embedded into a company, switching costs are high, and it actually pays off to stay with the company, as more time and data make the software even better. This is exactly what happened with the first operating systems for computers, which today still dominate the market.

A Trillion Dollar Opportunity

Palantir has one of the largest TAMs out there right now, which we can separate into two key areas.

Firstly, US defence spending could reach over $1 trillion by 2030. As we’ve seen recently, this is a trend that continues to accelerate.

Palantir stands out as a key player in terms of U.S. defense software, but it could also play an important role in other forms of spending, like more general government AI infrastructure.

And this isn’t limited to the US. Just recently, the UK pledged to spend $1 billion on Palantir services.

Just on the government side, that’s arguable—well over $1 trillion—and then we have to add the accelerating commercial segment.

Let’s look at Palantir through the lens of Enterprise Software.

This market is projected to reach over $500 billion by 2030, according to Grand View Research. Again, consider that this is a market dominated by a few players but ready for disruption, and Palantir is making a splash.

How will Palantir do this?

Ultimately, it hinges on the success of its signature platforms: Gotham, Foundry, and AIP, which is an extension of Foundry.

Gotham is the system used by the government, which provides data analytics in a secure environment. It's had increasing adoption throughout the years, and as mentioned before, there is a high degree of customer lock-in here.

And what better customer to lock in than the US government?

On the other hand, Foundry and AIP have been a huge hit over the last year. We've seen commercial revenues accelerate, and this is what's driving the narrative.

At 1 customer, 2 human FDEs spawned an army of AI FDEs to migrate a customer off their legacy data warehouse in 5 days, something that would have taken an army of SIs up to 2 years. This is not a prototype. This is production across our customers, the results are shocking.

Source: Earnings Call

These are exponential improvements in operations, and it's only logical to believe revenues could now grow exponentially too for the commercial segment.

In short, we have a huge TAM, both in government and enterprise, a clear moat around the business, and revenues that will convert into earnings at a high margin.

Risks

Of course, this isn’t news to anyone, and Palantir is already very richly priced, which leads to a high risk that execution doesn’t meet the high expectations.

Compared to its peers, Palantir trades at a valuation completely detached from fundamentals. But ultimately, the bull thesis hinges on the idea that Palantir has no peers, at least for now

Which brings us to the other risk factor, which is competition.

Competition is going to accelerate, and while Palantir is more insulated in the government sector, the commercial sector could still be disputed.

Final Thoughts

AI has matured, and as such, it’s time to invest in the companies that are actually creating profit for it, not just spending billions laying down the infrastructure.

Palantir is a clear winner, priced as such, but in my opinion, still very much worth holding for the long term as growth and earnings compound.

data.title: 2026 Inside Real Estate Outlook: A Cycle For Selectivity
data.description:
Overview

A market defined by divergence is ripe for alpha

Policy shocks, persistent growth. While 2025 is a year that tested the resilience of the global economy, it should be remembered as one of unmatched resilience in the face of headwinds and uncertainty. Trade tensions deepened, inflationary pressures lingered, and geopolitical fractures widened. Yet, against this backdrop of uncertainty, growth endured - powered by strong balance sheets, agile policy responses, and the accelerating march of artificial intelligence. These forces not only defined 2025 but they will also shape the contours of 2026. The United States sits at the epicentre of both global growth hopes and concerns.

The commercial real estate (CRE) cycle has moved into recovery by nearly all traditional measures. REIT valuations - often a bellwether for cyclical turning points - have risen broadly since their 2023 trough.

Valuations across most major indices have stabilized, and credit markets are functioning again, helping bolster transaction activity. At the same time, distress - a classic lagging indicator - continues to edge higher, reflecting the long tail of the downturn.

A rising tide will no longer lift all boats. Returns are diverging sharply across sectors, regions, and fund strategies. This unevenness isn’t a flaw in the cycle - it is the cycle. Investors are no longer confronting a broad-based downturn; they are navigating widening dispersion. We believe the cycle ahead will be alpha-driven and real estate investors will increasingly need to take a page from equity markets, where asset and market selection drive performance.

Income will be the dominant driver of returns over the next 12 to 18 months. Unlike the post global financial crisis (GFC) period - when returns were amplified by accommodative policy and financial engineering - the next phase of performance will hinge on property fundamentals and effective asset management. Net operating income will likely drive both income returns and capital returns in a higher-for-longer rate backdrop where cap rate compression is limited. Structural themes will remain important, but clearing return hurdles requires sharper focus and execution.

Portfolios will become more globally diversified. Heightened geopolitical uncertainty and a broader push for global diversity are prompting investors to rethink geographic concentration. While the U.S. remains too large and too deep to overlook, investors are becoming more selective. Europe benefits from improving economic conditions, wider cap rates, and lower sovereign debt yields, making leverage more accretive. And Asia is drawing growing attention as investors recognize how under-allocated they are relative to the region’s share of the global investable CRE universe.

Macroeconomic overview

Global growth resilience amid political upheaval

  • Despite notable policy and geopolitical shocks this year, the global economy has proven resilient. Policy volatility has eased, uncertainty has receded, and AI-driven capex has accelerated - supporting a more constructive outlook. As a result, as shown in the chart on the right, consensus GDP forecasts for 2025 have been revised higher across the U.S., Euro area, and China, reversing the downgrades seen around “Liberation Day.”
  • U.S. projections for 2026 have also been revised upward to 1.9%, likely reflecting anticipated Fed rate cuts and the impact of the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). By comparison, the Eurozone stands at 1.1% vs 1.4% in 2025 and China is at 4.4% vs 4.9% in 2025.
  • Still, early-2025 policy disruptions have left lasting marks. Tariffs are reshaping global trade dynamics, likely keeping inflation elevated and persistent. Meanwhile, lingering concerns around U.S. institutional stability and its geopolitical posture may have structurally weakened the U.S. dollar.
  • These pressures are compounded by rising global fiscal concerns, which threaten to keep long-term government borrowing costs elevated, limiting the scope for future fiscal stimulus, and potentially undermining the effectiveness of monetary policy. The near-term picture is robust, but underlying vulnerabilities are forming.

Economic stability could be on the horizon for many nations

The U.S. average effective tariff rate has declined to roughly 17%. Although this is the highest level since the 1940s, it remains well below the 25–30% range feared immediately after Liberation Day in April 2025. As a result, the estimated drag on U.S. GDP is about 1%, compared with worst-case projections earlier this year of roughly 2.6%.

Pass-through to inflation has been muted, as firms have absorbed much of the cost through margin compression, supply chain adjustments, and strategic sourcing. Even so, lower-income households are bearing a disproportionate share of the remaining price pressures.

That said, it's still too early to dismiss the longer-term effects of tariffs, corporate margins are near all-time highs, accommodating lower pass throughs today, and the Fed will need to stay vigilant to evolving inflation risks.

…The U.S. Tax Bill ('OBBBA') provides a boost to growth

The passage of the “One Big Beautiful Bill Act” OBBBA, which cuts taxes for many businesses and consumers, meaningfully offsets the contractionary effects of trade tariffs, which are effectively a tax increase on consumers.

Personal income tax refunds could even deliver another round of stimulus in 2026, boosting demand, although benefits will be concentrated among higher earners.

The second-round effects of the OBBBA remain underappreciated: if business tax cuts lead to productivity-enhancing corporate capex and concomitant wage increases, they could generate significant multiplier effects for the broader economy, potentially shifting the net fiscal impact into stimulative territory.

AI is an additional growth engine, but downside risks should be monitored

“When people get very excited, as they are today about artificial intelligence... every experiment gets funded. Every company gets funded. The good ideas and the bad ideas, and investors have a hard time in the middle of this excitement distinguishing between the good ideas and the bad ideas. And so that’s also probably happening today.

But it doesn’t mean that anything that’s happening isn’t real.”JeffBezos,Founder,Amazon,2025

In the first half of 2025, Artificial Intelligence (AI)-related capital expenditure accounted for nearly half of GDP growth, a significant figure that underscores the scale of this structural shift. With technology firms showing no appetite to rein in spending, AI will remain a dominant force in the year ahead.

AI bubble concerns are likely overdone, but questions around valuations, return on investment, and productivity payoffs will persist. The narrative is shifting. A decline in AI-related spending is a risk to the U.S. economy given its recent contributions to GDP.

Overall resilience conceals a K-shaped economy

U.S. economy is not without fault lines

Its K-shaped nature - where affluent households drive consumption while lower-income cohorts struggle - poses a structural challenge even if the aggregate impact remains muted.

As the mid-term election year approaches, the Trump administration is paying closer attention to these lower-income segments, seeking to ease affordability pressures through potential cash transfers and more lenient tariff policies in select sectors.

While fiscal constraints may limit full implementation, the government’s focus on households at the lower rungs of the income and wealth distributions is likely to remain a central element of policy.

Labor market steady in a low turnover environment

The job market remains steady for now, but its dynamics will also warrant close attention.

Hiring has slowed, but layoffs remain contained - although announced firings have increased - and unemployment remains near historic lows. Labor demand is slowing but shifting labor supply dynamics, such as tighter immigration policies, are also a significant factor in lower net payroll creation.

While we believe that reduced hiring signals a cooling in labor market activity, we are not on the brink of a job's recession or unravelling.

AI adoption could also reshape employment over time, but in the near-term, firms view it as a growth catalyst rather than a threat. The longer-term implications for labor displacement and wage dynamics, however, remain an open question.

Rate cuts: Insurance, not recession

For the U.S. Federal Reserve, the interplay of AI- driven investment, fiscal stimulus, and labor supply dynamics points to a more moderate interest rate easing cycle.

Price stability remains in range, but inflation continues to run hot, and the full impact of tariffs on price pressures is still unfolding – warranting a more measured approach.

In 2026, the Fed’s key challenge will be calibrating policy in an environment where structural, rather than cyclical, forces increasingly shape growth and inflation dynamics.

That said, for CRE, long-term interest rates are the key driver, not the Fed funds rate. We see limited potential for a significant drop in 10-year Treasury yields, and a fall below 4% may not spark the relief rally some anticipate, as it could signal a weaker economic environment. What matters most is stability in 10-year yields. A range of 4.0% to 4.25%, with inflation around 3%, would provide a highly supportive backdrop for CRE.

Key themes shaping the markets

"It is the ability to make predictions that is the basis of intelligence."

Jeff Hawkins

A complex macro backdrop requires a back-to-basics investment approach

If the global macro landscape feels unusually complex and disorderly, that’s because it is. We’re not imagining the heightened uncertainty - we're witnessing multiple structural transformations colliding and interacting in real-time, creating a genuinely more complex and volatile environment than we’ve seen in recent decades. Over the next few pages, we discuss some of the key themes we believe will have the most significant impact on investor strategies in 2026.

In this environment, we believe real estate investors should focus on what they can control: driving net operating income growth. Achieving this will require disciplined property, market, and fund selection, as return dispersion across real estate continues to widen - the central theme for 2026 and beyond.

A rising tide lifts boats unevenly

Dispersion in returns is widening as structural shifts in demand drivers, capital flows, and local fundamentals reshape markets. Demographics, regulation, and infrastructure investment are further amplifying gaps between markets that once moved in tandem. In this environment, informed selection is essential to outperform.

We analyzed prices using Green Street data for each property sector across the top 50 U.S. markets. Our analysis shows that, for every sector except office and self-storage, prices in the top-quartile markets have increased more than 10% since their respective troughs.

The NCREIF ODCE Index, a benchmark tracking 25 core open-end funds invested in U.S. real estate, reveals the top quartile funds have generated nearly 6% total returns over the prior year versus just under half a percent for the bottom quartile.

And in Europe, single sector funds are outperforming their multi-sector peers reflecting that selectivity is driving performance.

Dollar weakness: Cyclical pressures, structural concerns

Fears of a U.S. recession earlier this year, driven by policy uncertainty and volatility, had weighed on the U.S. dollar. Although these fears proved unfounded, persistent hedging against the greenback suggests lingering aversion to dollar exposure.

Indeed, concerns over the credibility and independence of U.S. institutions, including the Federal Reserve, have persisted, contributing to the U.S. dollar’s sharp slide this year. In conjunction with these cyclical and structural dynamics, gold’s surge reflects its appeal as a hedge against both U.S. policy uncertainty and upside inflation risks.

While the U.S. dollar’s demise should not be exaggerated - after all, it remains the world’s reserve currency given the unmatched depth and liquidity of U.S. capital markets - its share of global reserves continues to erode amid rising institutional concerns.

From partnership to regional spheres of influence

Geopolitical competition between regional powers is deepening across strategic technologies, energy, supply chains, and legislations. These shifts are reshaping global trade flows and investment priorities, creating ripple effects across real estate markets.

As trade realigns along spheres of influence, cross-border capital flows may favor markets with resilient infrastructure and energy security. Investors should monitor how regional trade blocs influence sector performance and pricing.

Property type implications:

  • Industrial & logistics: Trade flows are increasingly strained by volatile policy and shifting alliances, creating uncertainty for global supply chains. This unpredictability is influencing industrial and logistics demand patterns, with investors prioritizing markets that offer regulatory stability and resilient infrastructure.
  • Data centers: Strategic technology rivalry and AI adoption are accelerating demand for hyperscale and cloud infrastructure, particularly in regions with secure energy access.
  • Office & lifesciences: Nations prioritizing tech innovation and research ecosystems will see stronger fundamentals for high-quality office and lab space, while weaker regions risk prolonged vacancy.

In the age of AI, the biggest barrier to progress is energy

The rapid rise of AI has fueled extraordinary investment and valuations, but history suggests early phases of transformative technologies often experience bubbles. If earnings fail to meet lofty expectations, credit ratings could deteriorate - raising financing costs and pressuring exit cap rates. Public equity markets are likely to feel this impact more acutely than private real estate, but CRE is not immune, especially for assets tied to tech-driven demand.

Volatility in capital markets can spill into real estate pricing, particularly for sectors dependent on technology tenants or specialized infrastructure.

An AI bubble would not signal the end of the trend - it’s part of the maturation process. Investors who remain disciplined and selective can benefit from market inefficiencies and position for long-term growth as the industry stabilizes.

New trading paradigm provides optionality

Global trade affects more than just imports and exports - it also drives capital flows in commercial real estate. Overall transaction volumes in 2025 are up across most regions, though activity varies by country. The U.S., Germany, and Australia are experiencing strong markets, while Canada, the UK, and China are softer, reflecting a mix of domestic and foreign investment. On the cross-border front, the UK has emerged as the top destination for overseas investors, with foreign investment totaling over $22 billion in the first nine months of 2025, an increase of more than 12% over last year. The U.S. has remained resilient, seeing continued growth in foreign capital at 6%, while Hong Kong, Australia, Germany, and Japan have recorded some of the largest percentage gains globally. Earlier in 2025, some speculated that European real estate could attract North American capital amid rising geopolitical uncertainty, but that shift has yet to materialize.

Global migration fuels CRE - but policies are tightening

Global Gateway markets have historically thrived on international migration because it fuels labor supply, consumer demand, and cultural diversity, all of which underpin commercial real estate growth. Migrants often cluster in urban hubs with strong economic ecosystems, driving absorption in multifamily housing, retail, and hospitality sectors. Skilled migration corridors from India, China, and the Philippines to the U.S. and Europe also reinforce demand for office space in technology and healthcare clusters, while displaced populations create urgent needs for flexible housing and community infrastructure. For investors, these flows translate into higher occupancy rates, resilient retail sales, and logistics demand tied to growing consumption.

However, the rise of nationalism and tightening immigration policies in several advanced economies pose a structural risk to these dynamics. Political pressure to restrict inflows, whether through visa caps, stricter asylum rules, or labor market protectionism, could slow population growth in gateway cities. This will have a dampening effect on demand for housing and reducing the talent pipeline that supports office and innovation hubs. In markets like the U.S. and parts of Europe, uncertainty around immigration reform may delay development projects or shift capital toward domestic migration-driven regions.

State of CRE cycle

A top-down view of the forces shaping CRE

After two years of price resets, the recovery is now underway

  • REITs have rallied from their 2023 lows, and valuations across major global indices appear to have troughed. Yet distress, a lagging indicator, continues to edge higher. Measures of sentiment in both the U.S. and Europe shows confidence is on the upswing.
  • The debt markets are open and liquid. U.S. CRE originations volumes are 47% higher year-to-date compared with the same period in 2024; every lender type increased originations. And in Europe, financing has emerged as the lead sub-indicator of the INREV Consensus Indicator.
  • Transaction volumes in the U.S. were 17% higher in 2025 compared to the same period in 2024. European volumes are still finding their footing as green shoots emerge.
  • Private equity CRE returns in both the U.S. and Europe are expected to normalize higher, but, beneath the surface, returns are diverging sharply across property types, geographies, and funds.

Source: Principal Real Estate, December 2025.

Global real estate valuations are rising

U.S. listed returned 4.5% year-to-date as of the end of November. Dispersion is pronounced as there’s a nearly 55-percentage point spread between the best and worst performing property types reinforcing that property selection matters.

European REITs produced solid returns of 25.5% on a USD basis and 12% on a EUR basis. This may reflect rising demand for European real estate against a stronger economic backdrop.

Unlevered property returns in the U.S. stood at 3.8% YTD as of 3Q25 vs 4.9% in Europe. This is consistent with our original projections of 5% unlevered total returns in the U.S., driven primarily by income returns. We have argued Europe may be close to 6% to 7% given a combination of an improving economic outlook, wider cap rates, and declining interest rates.

Source: Bloomberg, NCREIF, INREV, Principal Real Estate. Private real estate data as of 3Q25; Listed REITs as of 30 November 2025.

The CRE market appears to be progressing through a typical reset

Source: Bloomberg, NCREIF, Principal Real Estate, 3Q25.

CRE credit distress: More cycle than crisis

While concerns around commercial real estate (CRE) credit are growing, the data suggests a market that is adjusting, not breaking down. Loan modifications have increased, but remain modest relative to total exposure, and the share of maturing CMBS loans being paid off remains close to long-term historical averages.

Although delinquency rates and distress are likely to rise, CRE debt distress is typically a lagging indicator. Historically, REITs hit bottom first, followed by private CRE valuations 12 to 18 months later, and delinquencies 12 to 24 months after that. This cycle seems no different. REITs are up roughly 35% from their 2023 lows, valuations are recovering, yet distress is still rising — a sign the market is working through its reset, not breaking down.

Source: MSCI / RCA, Principal Real Estate, 3Q25.

Easing lending standards is a tailwind

CRE lending markets are open and liquid

The volume of U.S. CRE loan originations is up 47% year-to-date in 3Q25 compared with the same period in 2024; every lender type increased originations. The index stands at 354, which is 75% higher than its historical average since 1Q 2002. There have only been four other times that the index has stood at a higher level.

At the same time, the Federal Reserve’s 3Q25 Senior Loan Officer Opinion Survey showed unchanged lending standards for banks and the first increase in CRE loan demand since 1Q22. This marks a notable shift in the CRE market after several years of tightening standards and declining loan demand. It suggests potential for accelerating price increases ahead.

In Europe, financing is the lead sub indicator of the INREV Consensus Indicator. At a reading of 67.3 it represents the strongest level recorded across sub indicator since inception in March 2023.

Source: Federal Reserve, INREV, Principal Real Estate, 4Q 2025.

U.S. transactions rise; green shoots emerge in Europe

U.S. CRE transactions for the first nine months of 2025 were 17% higher than during the same period in 2024. Transaction volumes often serve as a barometer of market sentiment: accelerating volumes signal growing demand and confidence, while slowing volumes suggest increasing caution. Today’s market still faces notable risks and uncertainties, yet the recent pickup in transaction activity, reminiscent of 2014, is particularly noteworthy.

European CRE volumes show that the market is still finding its footing. Transaction activity fell -3% year-over-year in Q3 2025 and, year-to-date, volumes were flat. The disconnect versus the U.S. is unusual, as historically CRE transaction volumes in the U.S. and Europe have moved in tandem, and when gaps emerge, they tend to normalize quickly.

Signs are now emerging that Europe may be on the cusp of doing just that. The number of European transactions under contract in October reached its highest level since 2022, and the Q3 2025 INREV Consensus Survey showed a meaningful rebound in investor sentiment, driven by improved financing conditions and increasing market liquidity.

Source: MSCI / RCA, INREV, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025.

Sentiment meaningfully improves

The Real Estate Roundtable's 3Q25 Sentiment Index shows that 56% believe it is a more favorable environment than a year ago and 34% believe conditions remain the same. Looking towards the future, 73% of participants express confidence in more favorable market conditions emerging a year from now.

Europe is also seeing a meaningful shift in sentiment. Momentum was broad-based across the index, but financing conditions led the way, with the sub-indicator reaching 70.1, the highest reading ever recorded by the index. Investment liquidity also showed a sharp rebound while leasing and operations remain on solid footing.

Source: Real Estate Roundtable, INREV, Principal Real Estate, December 2025.

U.S. CRE private equity returns are normalizing higher

Source: NCREIF, Preqin, Principal Real Estate, 30 September 2025.

Dispersion in core fund returns likely to persist in U.S. and Europe

Source: NCREIF, INREV, Principal Real Estate, 30 September 2025.

European single sector funds outperform

Focused strategies in Europe are pulling ahead of diversified funds

The broader INREV All-Fund Index has delivered trailing 4-quarter total returns of 4.4% so far in 2025 across all strategies.

This is driven in large part by single sector funds that are outperforming their multi-sector peers. For instance, residential has generated trailing 4-quarter returns of 7.7% followed by industrial at 5.4% and retail at 4.8%. By comparison, multi-sector funds have risen by a more modest 3%.

Single sector office funds are the notable laggard, declining nearly 1%, owing largely to a structural shift that has yet to fully normalize following the pandemic.

Source: INREV, Principal Real Estate, 3Q25

Strategic outlook

Insights from our quadrant leaders

Source: Principal Real Estate, December 2025

Debt still dominates, but Equity begins to return

The recovery is taking hold, though it is likely to be more measured than in past cycles driven by greater dispersion. We continue to view 2026 as an attractive vintage for several emerging investment strategies, with performance increasingly tied to disciplined asset and market selection supported by durable secular drivers.

REITs underperformed in 2025 but remain well-positioned to capitalize on pricing dislocations across broader CRE markets in 2026. Public real estate continues to trade at more attractive levels than private equity and offers valuable diversification—particularly as equity market performance becomes increasingly concentrated in a small group of AI-driven corporates.

The lending environment has improved significantly heading into 2026, with credit markets fully open and commercial banks back in the market. A heavy maturity schedule and rising sales volumes are providing broad support to the recovery across strategies.

Resilience has been the hallmark of the CMBS market through the correction and into the early stages of recovery. Issuance is expected to post another increase in 2026, building on a strong 2025. As in the private markets, activity will be supported by elevated maturities and resurgent investment demand. Despite tighter spreads, CMBS continues to offer competitive yields.

Source: Principal Real Estate, December 2025

Active selection can add alpha within real estate

Performance among sectors in a given year can vary widely. Dynamic allocation may provide investors the best possible outcomes by maximizing the opportunity set in any given environment

Average performance spread across quadrants since 2007: 18.8%

Performance gross total return

Source: Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025. Indices: US REITs: FTSE NAREIT All Equity REIT Index; IG CMBS: Bloomberg US CMBS Investment Grade Index; Private RE Debt: Giliberto-Levy Index; Private RE Equity: ODCE variable-weight index. Past performance is not indicative of future performance. Indices are unmanaged and do not take into account fees, expenses, and transaction costs and it is not possible to invest in an index. See important information for index descriptions.

Early-cycle strength meets K-shaped selectivity

The private real estate market entered a recovery phase in 2025 as pricing firmed, and liquidity returned. That upswing continues in 2026, fueled by attractive below-replacement-cost valuations, resilient NOI growth, and lending markets that are once again open for business.

Long-dated Treasury yields, however, suggest private equity real estate is unlikely to see the classic post-GFC V-shaped recovery. Instead, this cycle will be marked by sharper dispersion across markets and sectors—driven by asset quality rather than financial engineering—requiring investors to sharpen their focus.

We expect manager differentiation to widen as improving price discovery flows through to performance and liquidity generation. Depth of knowledge and experience across sectors and geographies will increasingly define outperformance.

Preferred sectors remain those benefiting from long-term secular trends, including technology and demographics. Key examples are data centers, diverse rental housing formats, and modern logistics facilities. We also favor open-air retail centers anchored by non-discretionary retailers, which have shown resilient cash flows throughout the downturn.

Source: NCREIF, Principal Real Estate, 30 September 2025.

Diversification with REITs has rarely looked so attractive

The Magnificent Seven now account for more than 30% of the S&P 500’s market capitalization—and an even larger share of the tech-heavy NASDAQ. While AI may reshape the future, many investors already carry significant exposure to AI-linked megacaps through broad index holdings. In our view, diversification remains essential for long-term success, especially as market leadership continues to rotate over time.

REITs provide a compelling alternative with low correlations to these mega-cap tech names, offering a differentiated return profile. This makes REITs an attractive option for investors seeking both liquidity and the stability of real assets in a portfolio. Importantly, this diversification comes at a historical discount today, with the earnings multiple spread between REITs and equities at record lows compared to the last 20 years.

Left chart: As of 30 September 2025. Source: FactSet. Right chart: As of 31 October 2025. Source: FactSet. Enterprise Value (EV) to EBITDA price multiples are used to measure valuations. The spread plotted is the EV/EBITDA of the FTSE EPRA/NAREIT Developed index minus the EV/EBITDA of the MSCI World index. Expensive and cheap valuations are represented by spreads higher or lower than one standard deviation from the mean, respectively. Fair value is represented by valuations between one standard deviation from the mean. The average is since 28 February 2005. Indices are unmanaged and do not take into account fees, expenses, and transaction costs and it is not possible to invest in an index.

CRE debt offers compelling risk-adjusted returns

Lending strength improved measurably in 2025, with originations through 3Q25 up 47% from the same period in 2024. Credit markets are fully open, and with banks re-entering the market, all lending cohorts are deploying capital. Notably spreads have compressed, especially in construction and bridge lending.

A steep remaining maturity schedule and ongoing momentum in sales transactions will continue to generate more attractive lending opportunities over the next 12 months. These dynamics, however, depend on continued stability in 10-year Treasury yields and an accommodative Federal Reserve providing some relief to the SOFR rate.

The office sector, while still cautionary, is showing meaningful improvement in both fundamentals and liquidity, albeit from a low base. Yet, the office sector remains one asset where quality is paramount, making selectivity critical. Retail and alternative sectors are poised to maintain strong lender interest as investors seek diversification after years of heavy allocations to multifamily and industrial assets.

Conservative LTVs on property prices that have already reset help to mitigate against further declines in property prices. It is important to note, however, that CRE mortgages are secured by income-producing real estate. We believe CRE debt plays a key role in portfolio optimization for investors seeking to reduce volatility and drawdown risk.

Source: NCREIF/CREFC, Preqin, Principal Real Estate, Q3 2025. This reflects our tactical views on relative opportunities over a 6–12-month horizon. 1 Preqin Private Capital Indices 2 NCREIF/CREFC Open End Debt Fund Aggregate.

Stronger issuance; greater credit dispersion and tighter spreads

The CMBS market has proved resilient, and we anticipate another productive year in 2026. Issuance is projected to increase another 18% over the next twelve months, extending 2025’s record private-label volumes. Growth in SASB* issuance—including more fixed-rate deals— and a recovering CRE CLO market are expected to remain key themes.

Demand will be reinforced by approximately $93 billion in 2026 maturities, including the first five-year conduit and floating-rate SASB loans from 2021. Still, credit performance will stay uneven: conduit delinquencies, elevated in 2025 due to office and multifamily, are likely to rise further as office maturities pressure refinancing and multifamily fundamentals normalize.

Conduit loan maturity payoff rates trended higher in 2025 overall, but it remains property-type specific. Industrial and retail payoff rates remained strong in 2025, while less than half of office loans refinanced. Lower expected rates and stronger lending and transaction activity should lift overall payoff rates in 2026, even as office stress keeps headline delinquencies high.

Despite tighter spreads versus corporate bonds, CMBS continues to offer competitive yield, and the SASB market remains an appealing alternative and complement to private real estate debt given its liquidity.

Source: JPMorgan CMBS Weekly Datasheet, Principal Real Estate, 21 November 2025. *SASB = Single-Asset, Single-Borrower

Sector opportunities

State of the commercial real estate sectors

Source: Principal Real Estate, December 2025.

Global data centers (High conviction)

Prioritizing cloud and AI inference; maintaining caution around GenAI

AI adoption has accelerated globally, with U.S. private AI investment reaching $109B in 2024, including $37B directed toward AI infrastructure, which is reshaping data center development. While vacancy in established hubs is at historic lows, a new speculative wave—driven by generative AI (GenAI) facilities built in emerging markets ahead of actual utilization—introduces growing demand-forecast risk.

Investors should prioritize cloud and AI inference data centers within established availability zones, where power, network density, and tenant pipelines support more durable cash flows. Going forward, new developments must balance cost discipline with designing flexible, future-proof facilities that can adapt to evolving technologies and regulatory standards.

Source: NVCA, Pitchbook, Moody’s Analytics, Stanford University HAI, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025.

U.S. residential (High conviction)

A mismatch in housing options creates dislocation on a regional basis

The U.S. housing market is shaped less by an overall shortage of units and more by a persistent mismatch between the types and locations of housing and the evolving needs of households.

Rentership, which accounts for roughly 35% of households, is a central feature, but it extends far beyond the traditional Class A apartments that dominate new construction, encompassing single-family rental homes, manufactured housing, and even senior, as well as student living options.

An optimal nationwide solution is elusive and suggests that understanding local market dynamics is critical. Targeted policies and investments that align new supply with actual demand, both in terms of housing type and location, are essential to building a more balanced, accessible, and resilient housing system for all Americans.

Source: Joint Center for Housing Studies, US Census Bureau, 2021 American Community Survey 5-Year Estimates.

Europe residential (High conviction)

Undersupply, stability, and rebalancing

European residential property has shown great resilience in recent years, supported by a persistent structural undersupply of housing across major European markets.

Demand will remain robust through 2026 and beyond, driven by migration flows, evolving household structures, continued urbanization, and longer rental tenures, as affordability pressures delay homeownership for young adults and lower- to middle-income households living in metropolitan areas.

At the same time, institutional capital is poised to deepen its footprint in the sector, as investors rebalance portfolios and seek counter-cyclical and income-driven assets with strong structural fundamentals.

[1] Excludes borrowing costs

Source: OECD, Principal Real Estate, Q2 2025.

Europe industrial (Selective conviction)

Logistics divide between future-proof and older stock solidifies

The European industrial and logistics market appears to have normalized. Headline metrics such as vacancy, take- up, and rental growth have softened, and overall performance has broadly converged with the other traditional property sectors.

Beneath the surface, however, a more nuanced and differentiated outlook is emerging. Occupier demand is increasingly selective, favoring modern, efficient, and future-proof assets. We expect the quality premium to continue widening, driven by three key factors:

  1. Rapid technological advancements in AI and warehouse robotics;
  2. Tightening sustainability standards;
  3. Limited new supply expected to enter the market in 2026.
Source: PMA, Principal Real Estate, December 2025. * = Estimate

U.S. industrial (Selective conviction)

Uneven rent growth requires investor selectivity

The U.S. industrial sector has emerged from the correction that began in 2022 with early signs of recovery, but only the top performing assets are still experiencing attractive rent growth.

A significant pipeline reset, with projects under construction down nearly 70% from the 2023 peak, is bringing supply back toward historical norms and paving the way for a healthier 2026–2027 development cycle.

Demand remains concentrated in top-tier, modern logistics facilities, while older assets continue to experience occupancy losses. This bifurcation is reflected in performance, with post-2010 buildings generating nearly double the returns of legacy properties over the past year at 6.3%, supporting our selectivity thesis.

We believe the evolving dynamics present an opportunity for industrial development for well-capitalized investors. While rising costs will remain a challenge, the current scarcity of new construction offers an attractive window in 2026–27, as limited deliveries meet renewed demand for modern logistics space.

U.S. retail (Selective conviction)

Necessity-based retail set to outperform the rest of the sector

While institutional investors remain underweight retail, the sector’s diversity and post-pandemic repositioning have made it a critical portfolio component. Cyclically resilient formats continue to attract investor attention, offering defensive positioning against e-commerce penetration. Consumer resilience, however, is being tested, as indicated by flattening sentiment and the potential impact of higher inflation, which could erode discretionary spending power for all but the top income groups.

Net demand remains positive, and availability rates are holding near cyclical lows. Limited development since the GFC has meaningfully reduced the investable universe. Although the U.S. has 116,000 shopping centers, less than 10% of total gross leasable area meets institutional-quality criteria, and demographic filters aligned with REIT standards reduce that share even further. This combination of healthy fundamentals and scarcity underpins our thesis that bifurcation is occurring within the retail sector.

U.S. healthcare (Selective conviction)

Poised to benefit from demographic-driven demand and limited supply

The U.S. healthcare sector continues to be supported by secular demographic trends. An aging population is fueling demand for higher levels of care and more diverse healthcare services.

Advanced age cohorts are expanding rapidly, creating sustained demand for healthcare facilities. Older patients also account for significantly higher healthcare utilization and spending, with individuals aged 65 and above far exceeding younger groups in annual doctor visits and healthcare costs.

The senior housing segment has largely recovered from its pandemic-related correction. Although new supply has increased, strong demand and high occupancy have made it one of the top-performing sectors.

Source: Census, Moody’s Analytics, Healthcare Realty, NIC, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025.

Europe hotels (Selective conviction)

Hidden opportunities in a maturing sector

Hotel performance has been strong over the past three years, with occupancy and average daily rates (ADR) rebounding sharply from a very low base.

Looking ahead, we expect a more moderate pace of improvement as room rates have already increased sharply, and tourism patterns return to their long-term growth trajectory.

In this environment, the most attractive opportunities lie in acquiring neglected assets that have suffered years of underinvestment and a lack of professional management.

By upgrading and repositioning these properties to align with current guest expectations and hospitality trends, investors can unlock significant capital value gains and income growth.

Europe retail (Selective conviction)

Retail’s barbell reality

Retail real estate performance in Europe is increasingly polarized.

Prime high-street locations, luxury pitches, and top-tier malls have remained highly resilient through 2025, supported by strong tourism and continued strength in premium discretionary spending.

At the same time, convenience, food-anchored, and essential retail continue to perform well, benefiting from stable daily-needs spending and sustained footfall.

In sharp contrast, the mid-segment formats—including secondary locations, smaller schemes, and older malls—continue to struggle with elevated vacancies, weakening tenant demand, and rising risks of obsolescence or closure.

U.S. student housing (Neutral)

Resilient today, structural pressures tomorrow

The student housing sector remains generally healthy today, but headwinds are emerging. The space has become more crowded and competitive over the past decade; and enrollment trends at four-year institutions are flattening.

Performance is increasingly nuanced and dependent on both region and university. The Southeast, for example, continues to outperform, with major universities reporting occupancy rates in the 90% range.

Despite recent strength, we recommend a cautious approach. The sector has become more difficult to underwrite, and successful investment strategies now require elevated due diligence and strong deal-level economics.

Source: National Center for Education Statistics, Walker Dunlop, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025.

Europe healthcare (Neutral)

Healthcare regains a firmer footing

The European healthcare sector is in the early stages of recovery. Operators are benefiting from a stabilizing debt market, and strong pricing and occupancy levels that are now exceeding pre-pandemic norms.

Investment activity picked up with large portfolio deals pointing to growing confidence, particularly towards the UK and, to a lesser extent, Spain, and Germany, driven by improving operator fundamentals and limited new supply.

We expect structural demographic trends will continue to support the sector over the medium and long term.

Source: Aedifica Q3 Results, Eurostat, Principal Real Estate, December 2025.

Global office (Selective conviction)

Investors finding value after a period of declining valuations

Global office demand is showing signs of stabilization following a painful correction. The sector, however, remains far from regaining its prior form and appeal to investors.

Transaction activity is still muted, but lending to the U.S. office sector, a leading indicator, has rebounded +185% year-over-year and is nearly back to long-term historical averages.

Core investors remain cautions, but opportunistic buyers are increasingly targeting pricing dislocations. In particular, we see attractive opportunities in selected European markets with limited supply of modern assets and resilient occupier demand, including the City of London, central Paris, and Madrid CBD.

Source: MBA, Global survey of working arrangements - Stanford Institute for Economic Policy Research, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025.

Global life sciences (Caution)

Research is struggling without funding

Fundamentals in the life sciences sector remain under pressure following a wave of overdevelopment and limited demand for new space.

Nationwide R&D lab vacancies rose to 22.7% mid-year, according to Cushman & Wakefield, even as total leasing activity increased. Demand remains challenged, with net absorption still negative—driven largely by lease expirations despite higher leasing volumes.

Post-pandemic venture capital funding trends also remain soft, and cuts to NIH funding for university research centers have further strained the sector’s recovery outlook.

Source: NVCA, Pitchbook, Cushman & Wakefield, Principal Real Estate, 3Q 2025.

Conclusion: Strategic selection will be key to thriving in a recovering, but divergent market

Steering the boat through divergence-driven waters

1 - Structural shifts redefine opportunity

2025 marked a transition toward stability, but the next cycle will be shaped by divergence. Investors should expect uneven growth and focus on markets with resilient fundamentals. The CRE comeback will not lift all boats to the same degree.

2 - Focus remains on sectors that benefit from strong secular tailwinds

Data centers and residential sectors are experiencing structural headwinds that may cause pain in some markets, but demand drivers are still present, just more nuanced, creating opportunities for thoughtful investors.

3 - A disciplined approach to capital deployment

Liquidity is improving, yet pricing remains sensitive to credit conditions and policy volatility. Tactical opportunities in debt and secondary markets, combined with selective sector exposure, offer the best path to long-term performance.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ADMA Biologics: Business Update And My Price Target
data.description:
ADMA Biologics (ADMA) had been my favorite stock for quite some time. I provided extensive coverage on ADMA on Seeking Alpha, writing 9 articles between 2021 and 2022. My last article on ADMA was published in May 2022 and was entitled: ADMA Biologics: A Path to Profits Looks Clear. At the time of publishing, the stock was trading at $1.63, and the enterprise value of the company was just north of $300 million.

About ADMA

For those unfamiliar with ADMA, it is a biopharma company that has niche products that treat immune deficiencies and infectious diseases. Several years ago, ADMA was not operating at the scale necessary to be profitable, but it has recently turned profitable, and the stock has reacted accordingly, increasing from the $2 range to its current price, around $17, with an enterprise value north of $4 billion.

At this time, I think ADMA remains slightly undervalued, but it does not present the same risk/reward profile that it once did. With that being said, I could see a scenario where ADMA is bought out at a much higher price and believe that management's latest business update may be indicating that they are thinking of that possibility and positioning the company for that scenario, or at the very least, aiming for investors to re-rate it as a company with a less capital-intensive business model. In this article, I will quickly dissect ADMA's latest results and guidance, which were provided on Monday. I will also discuss what initially attracted me to ADMA, give an overview of current valuation, and provide my price target.

Results and Guidance

On the top line, ADMA's estimate of its revenue for FY 2025 was $510M-$511M, coming in right above its guidance of $510M+. On the bottom line, ADMA reiterated Adjusted Net Income and Adjusted EBITDA guidance at $158M and $235M, respectively.

In terms of guidance, ADMA guided for revenue in FY 2026, 2027, and 2029 to be $635M, $775M, and greater than $1.1B, respectively. Adjusted EBITDA in those same periods was guided to be $360M, $455M, and at least $700M, respectively. Adjusted Net Income in FY 2026 and 2027 was guided to be $255M and $315M, respectively; no FY 2029 Adjusted Net Income guidance was provided.

Business Updates

In the business update, ADMA announced that (i) ASCENIV utilization accelerated, driven by record demand and expanding prescriber adoption (ii) it ended the fourth quarter with approximately $88M of total cash, representing approximately $40M of operating cash flow generated during the fourth quarter, (iii) it is in discussions with potential distributors to further diversify its commercial network, (iv) 2026 will provide for sustained gross margin expansion given that yield production moved into routine commercial execution during 2025 and (v) that a new drug in the pipeline development could provide a potential $300 - $500 million in annual revenue.

Regarding the yield enhancement, ADMA is the first company to receive FDA approval for a yield enhancement manufacturing strategy. As discussed at the JP Morgan Healthcare Conference, this process allows them to extract ~20% more finished goods from the same starting plasma, which provides more product to sell from the raw material. Not only does this expand gross margins and profitability, but this also makes ADMA a more attractive target, which I'll discuss further below.

ADMA ended the quarter with $88 million in cash, which excludes the expected proceeds from sales of the plasma collection centers, so this cash position should expand in the coming quarters. There are a few options with this cash. With a pivot away from building plasma collection centers, ADMA does not need that cash for capex for new buildings. Instead, it could return money to shareholders via dividends and/or stock buybacks. Alternatively, ADMA currently has $70 million of debt outstanding held by JPM that has a current interest rate of 6.75% and matures in August 2028. This debt does not have a prepayment penalty, so ADMA could pay this all off in the coming quarters and increase net income by saving on interest payments. I would love to see ADMA pay off this debt and lock in those savings before pivoting to returning money to shareholders.

The guidance outlined earlier does not include products in ADMA's pipeline. The ASCENIV pediatric approval could come as soon as the first half of 2026, so near-term revenue could be generated from that. SG-001 is much earlier in the approval process and will be a few years away, so I'm not counting that potential in my valuation.

Source: ADMA Presentation

One interesting development announced is that ADMA entered into a purchase agreement for the divestiture of three plasma centers for total proceeds of $12M. After these divestitures, ADMA will continue to own and operate seven plasma collection centers, and the company will enter into long-term plasma supply agreements with the purchaser. The pivot to divesting these supply centers is a move to shift the company to a more capital-light model.

Although I do like business models that are not capital intensive, I was surprised by this development. In the past, ADMA prided itself on its vertically integrated business model that provided unparalleled ability for tight internal controls and consistency in its products. I am sure that management has found operators that they believe can maintain their standards, but I worry about that development in the future and hope management was not deluded by an investor that pitched them on this maneuver in order to potentially get a multiple re-rating. If their business is not financially impacted and this works as intended, it could be a home run for the company.

At the time of the writing of this article, the stock is down Monday morning more than 7% and is sitting well below the all-time highs that it was sitting at in the first half of 2025. I suspect part of the reason for the sell-off is driven by concerns about this pivot that was not telegraphed well ahead of time, as well as investors getting used to earnings beats, which did not happen this quarter with reiterated Adjusted EBITDA and Adjusted Net Income results.

Prior Coverage

I began my coverage on ADMA with an article on January 1, 2021. At that time, the stock was trading very poorly for a variety of reasons and was trading around $2 per share. The core of my thesis was based on the value of its assets compared to its debt. The company had plasma collection centers and other assets of around $220 million, and its enterprise value was the same. There was a path to profitability with prodigious revenue growth, and insider buying coupled with the adoption of a poison pill indicated to me that management shared that belief.

The irony of the development on Monday morning is that the primary reason I first invested in ADMA back in 2020 and 2021, the value of its plasma collection centers, will no longer be part of its business, possibly as soon as this year.

In addition, a lot has changed since my last article on ADMA that was published on Seeking Alpha (back on May 12, 2022). Back then, ADMA was still unprofitable, but I outlined my bullishness for why I thought they were about to turn a corner. I have not provided any updates to readers since 2022 on where I stood on ADMA and thought that Monday morning's news provided a great opportunity to do so.

Value

Valuing a biopharma/biotech company is very difficult to do since there are many idiosyncratic differences among peers. NYU Stern put together a database with data as of January 2026 for different industries, including 496 biotech companies. In that dataset, such firms with a positive EBITDA had an average EV to EBITDA of 15.78. Currently, ADMA's is about 12, representing a meaningful discount to the average.

Price Target

There are three analysts that cover ADMA (Cantor Fitzgerald, Raymond James, and Mizuho Securities), and they have price targets of $30, $32, and $32–$35, respectively. Without any huge catalysts on the horizon, my one-year price target is $21--which would put its EV to EBITDA right in line with the average of market peers. Last year, as ADMA traded well above my price target, I began unloading my position and held about one-third of my original position. I have a very low-cost basis in ADMA, so any time I sell shares, I have large tax bills. Given that, I plan to hold these shares for the time being.

M&A Candidate

In the past few years, M&A in the biopharma/biotech sector has been very robust and is predicted to remain so, especially given the sentiment that the FTC and DOJ are more friendly in this administration than in previous ones. One of the reasons for ADMA's pivot to divesting their plasma collection centers may be with a view towards attracting a suitor. ADMA is an attractive target with a niche product that demands robust reimbursements with attractive margins. A larger company could help to expand its distribution and really grow this business. However, potential acquirers likely do not want the headache that comes along with operating plasma collection centers, so that may have contributed to management's reasoning for the divestitures. I am not banking on an acquisition of ADMA in 2026, but I certainly would not be surprised.

Downside Scenario

The biggest risk I see in ADMA's future is with respect to its product not staying consistent. Patients on ADMA's products show great improvement, and given they need to stay on the products forever, ADMA enjoys strong recurring revenue. If the new operators of the plasma collection centers are not able to keep the product consistent, patients could seek alternatives, and that would be a terrible outcome, not only for the patients who will need to find new treatments but also for ADMA's future.

Conclusion

ADMA's stock trades in a range-bound manner and is currently trading at the low end of that range given the announcement this morning. I remain moderately bullish on ADMA and would not be surprised if it was bought out in 2026.

data.title: Cream Rises To The Top: 2 Top-Tier BDCs To Buy
data.description:
As BDC investors, we are mostly yield-chasers, who want to both enhance and diversify our portfolio current income streams. The emphasis on yield per definition means that we fall more on the category of value-investors rather than those who are focused on significant price gains like the Mag-7 / AI-driven world.

And if we unpack what value investor really means is that basically it is all about buying assets or securities at prices that are below their intrinsic values - i.e., bargain hunting. Also the yield aspect comes with that as the lower the multiple is for any security, usually the higher the yield gets (provided that payout profiles remain unchanged).

At the same time, I hope that you will agree with me, most BDC investors are here for durable income extraction. In other words, it is not only about yield maximization and portfolio income diversification, but also about being able to sleep well at night. At least for me, the objective to get as high yield as possible, while not sacrificing the sustainability factor. Granted, sometime I make slight deviations by opening tactical positions in higher risk / higher return cases, say, CION Investment Corporation (CION) and Crescent Capital BDC, Inc. (CCAP), but all of that is very limited and still underpinned by robust assessments.

However, there is an inherent conflict in this approach: yield maximization vs. quality and thus valuations. Most of my BDC bull picks are concentrated in the discounted area of BDC world as there simply higher yielding opportunities lie and, in my view, there are also better working ground for hitting the balance between yield and safety.

Consequently, I have underemphasized players that trade at meaningful premiums, especially those that trade at significantly richer valuations than their NAV (not just on a relative basis to peers).

Main Street Capital (MAIN) is probably the best example - the most expensive BDC, but one that has managed to produce extraordinary returns despite, at some point, trading 2 times NAV:

While I see how sometimes it might be quite counterintuitive for value and high-yield investors to put "buy below intrinsic value" mantra aside, I think that it would be wrong to ignore the above P/NAV BDC category all together.

Just as there is a merit of introducing a separate - tactical BDC trade - bucket to access outsized return potential, there is for sure a value of paying extra to enhance the long-term income sustainability factor.

Let me flesh out this though through the following 2 BDCs.

2 BDCs that are worth the price

First of all, we have to understand that BDCs that trade at premium to NAV are not that many: eight out of 50+ players. And if we zoom into these eight BDCs, then we will notice a huge valuation discrepancy between the top and bottom level. The one that are more skewed to the upside in terms of P/NAV multiple levels are internally managed. Those, which are not internally managed, but trade at premium to NAV have generally robust non-accrual statistics, top-tier earnings quality metrics and, generally, sustainable capital allocation frameworks.

But it is exactly the valuation-rich BDCs - internally managed ones - that tend to be bypassed by value-oriented investors due to the issue I described above - i.e., relative premium making bargain hunters anxious about paying for something more than what it is worth.

I will elaborate on two concrete examples that showcase why such kind of approach might be suboptimal. These examples are Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN) and Trinity Capital (TRIN).

Both of them are internally managed BDCs, which means that they do not have to incur neither fixed management fees (typically 1.5% of total book) nor incentive fees, which are around 15% that apply on a certain surplus income that exceeds the pre-defined hurdle rate (around 8%). It depends on BDC, but typically the total external management fees account for 15% - 20% of the total income.

So, because internally managed BDCs such as MAIN and TRIN do not have to absorb this cost item and apart from it all of the value-creation elements remain the same as for externally managed BDCs, it is only logical that the market arrives at relative premium valuations.

Furthermore, we have to also appreciate the alignment of interest aspect, where for internally managed BDCs the management team operate as direct employees and have stock option programs in place, which tend to be positive for long-term value creation.

And here is the thing: the presence of premiums over NAV allows MAIN and TRIN to access equity-financing at attractive terms. When the sector average indicates largely exhausted debt capacities and 100% base dividend coverage level, having an access to an accretive equity source is a major advantage.

It comes in extremely handy in the prevailing environment, when the M&A markets are opening up that offer an opportunity for BDCs to expand their loan books, which is basically one of the main tools to combat interest rate driven headwinds - i.e., offsetting negative price momentum with positive volume momentum so that the net effect is neutral or at least not that negative.

Apart from the internal management feature, there are multiple other drivers, which render these two BDCs decent buys.

For TRIN it is primarily about enjoying the industry-leading NII per share sensitivity to changes in base rates or SOFR. For example, a 100-basis point drop would lead to only 1% of a drop in the NII per share. This is so because of the embedded loan investment interest rate floors. On top of that, TRIN has a robust NII quality in terms of immaterial PIK. As of Q3, 2025, the PIK accounted for less than 1% of the total NII generation. The same applies to TRIN's non-accrual statistic. So, by acquiring TRIN, we are opening an exposure to high-quality BDC that carries an earnings profile, which is de-risked from unfavorable PIK and SOFR headwinds. And the best in all of this is that TRIN produces a monthly 12.9% dividend. Not bad deal at all.

MAIN's offer is a bit different and even more conservative (sustainable) than for TRIN. We can see that through ~7% yielding monthly dividend and much higher multiple - i.e., 1.90x vs. 1.19x (for MAIN and TRIN, respectively).

In MAIN's case we get a BDC, which has the fourth most conservative balance sheet in the entire space. The debt to equity is 0.73x, providing a solid basis for debt-driven growth as well, which is obviously even more accretive than funding that with equity (even though it can be sourced cheaply). In its portfolio MAIN has close to 10% high-quality equity exposure, where the lion's share of this book consists of cash-flowing and dividend producing assets. These are exactly the right targets to bring to the market for monetization, when the M&A and interest rate environments are so favorable. The benefit here is that equity-driven returns are inherently negatively correlated to interest rate changes as opposed to plain vanilla first lien loan investments. Finally, MAIN is a lower middle market BDC, where the spread compression has not been as pronounced battle as for the rest of the space due to the inherent constraints for large money managers to justify their time spend for small ticket size transactions (e.g., to move the needle for billion dollar portfolios, investing in $5 - $15 million loans is not that efficient, to say the least).

All in all, sometimes it is worth paying premium to get better quality. TRIN and MAIN are two such examples. Maybe in the short to medium-term they could underperform the index due to lack of discounted valuations, but in the long-run, which is what matters for me, the performance should be solid.
