data.title: Market Premium For 10-Year Yield Holds Near Fair Value Estimate
data.description:
The market premium for the US 10-year Treasury yield continues to trade near a fair value estimate, based on the average estimate of three models run by CapitalSpectator.com. Today’s update shows an ongoing extension of the near-neutral level for the benchmark rate in recent months, following several years of a high market premium.

The current estimate indicates that the 10-year yield traded roughly 22 basis points over fair value in December, based on analytics using monthly data. That’s close to the lowest market premium in three years.

The recent shift toward a neutral level continues to highlight the reversal of the previous sharp rise in the market premium during 2022-2024. The recent drop from the previous peak reminds us that valuation for the 10-year yield remains cyclical, albeit on an irregular and difficult-to-forecast timing basis.

Despite the seemingly random fluctuations in the market premium/discount through time, history suggests that the cycle revolves around zero (neutral). In turn, this dynamic provides an implied forecast for managing exposure to maturity risk for a bond-market strategy.

The current reading, which reflects a slight premium, implies a recommendation to adopt a more or less neutral stance for maturity risk vis-à-vis the 10-year yield.

By contrast, a higher allocation to maturity risk looked timely in late 2023, when the market premium was relatively high – above 120 basis points at the peak. Since then, the 10-year yield has fallen, and the bond market has rallied.

There’s no guarantee that the cycle will continue to repeat, although it does appear to rhyme. Betting otherwise still looks like an excessively risky assumption.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Disinflation Gains A Second Wind, As Forecast
data.description:
A disappointing earnings report from a bellwether in the banking sector set the tone for yesterday’s trading. JPMorgan stock slipped 4% after mixed results due to lower-than-expected investment banking fees. Financials were further pressured by President Trump’s vocal support for legislation to curb swipe fees charged by credit card issuers. Small-cap stocks bucked the trend in reaction to a better-than-expected Consumer Price Index (CPI) report for December. That combination left the major market indexes relatively flat with the exception of the Dow Jones Industrial Average, which was weighed down by its financial exposure.

The headline inflation number rose 0.3% for the month of December and 2.7% over the past year, in line with expectations, while the core rate rose a less-than-expected 0.2%, which resulted in the annualized rate holding steady at a four-year low of 2.6%. It was expected to increase to 2.8%. According to the Bureau of Labor Statistics, shelter costs rose 0.4% for the month and 3.2% over the past year, serving as the most significant factor in overall inflation. When we exclude shelter, the core CPI rose just 0.1% in December.

After a tariff-related spike last year, it appears that disinflation has gained a second wind and reasserted itself, as businesses absorbed most of the import costs rather than passing them on to consumers. There are some exceptions, with food prices being a sore spot for the American consumer. Food prices at home and away from home rose 0.7% last month. There were also some oddities in the report, such as the 3.3% drop in costs for wireless telephone services last month, which shaved 8 basis points off the core number. I know my Verizon bill didn’t go down 3.3% last month. I won’t quibble about the accuracy of the report. My interest is in how the market will respond and how consumers will react.

The bottom line is that the rate of overall price increases appears to be waning again, and even if there are inaccuracies in some line items, which I would not doubt, the most important one being shelter costs are still overstated in my view. This is primarily due to the way the BLS calculates the number, as I have iterated over the past several years. Rents are not rising at an annualized rate of 3%, as the report purports. Home prices are not rising at that rate either.

According to Apartment List, rents in December declined 0.8%, which is seasonal, while the more important year-on-year drop in rental rates was 1.3%. That is deflation, and its driver has been a surge in multifamily construction, with more than 600k new units on the market in 2024 and nearly half that number in the first half of last year. Immigration policy is also starting to have an impact with fewer renters in the market.

The Case-Shiller Home Price Index rose 1.3% in October, which was the lowest rate of price increase since July 2023, and I seriously doubt that rate is increasing given market dynamics in December.

A major criticism of the government’s calculation is that it does not account for homeowners’ insurance or property taxes, which have been on the rise, but I don’t think either offset the deflation we are seeing in rents and the negligible increase in home prices that exists today.

From the CPI report, we can derive inflation-adjusted (real) weekly take-home pay for workers, which increased 1.1% over the prior year in December. That is the same rate of increase we saw in December 2024. This is vitally important to the rate of consumer spending growth, which accounts for two-thirds of our overall rate of economic growth. Real income growth is the lifeblood of real consumer spending growth, which is what fuels our economic growth.

data.title: From Turbulence To Takeoff: Why Delta Could Become A Solid Dividend Grower
data.description:
Introduction

When it comes to classic dividend growth stocks, Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL) doesn't necessarily come to the mind of investors. However, 2026 and beyond could see the airline giant become a solid dividend grower to give your portfolio a liftoff.

DAL currently appears cheap with a forward multiple of less than 10x. With interest rates likely to continue declining and strategic growth initiatives, DAL may be worth considering if you're a long-term investor.

In this article, I discuss Delta's latest earnings, growth outlook, and why this dividend stock could become a solid dividend grower for the foreseeable future.

Previous Buy Thesis

I last covered Delta Air Lines, assigning the stock a Buy rating back in June. Despite turbulence, DAL has performed exceptionally well, up over 44% in the past 7 months.

They also surpassed my price target of $70.62 before the recent pullback. The airline giant significantly outperformed the S&P (SP500) over the same period, up 16%.

During their Q1 earnings, DAL managed to beat analysts' estimates on both their top and bottom lines. EPS amounted to $0.46, a beat by $0.07. Revenue beat by $580 million.

Despite this, DAL's headwinds were apparent as EPS remained flat from the prior year. Revenue did see some slight growth, up 3.3% to $13 billion.

Free cash flow was down from $1.4 billion to $1.3 billion. The company also announced a 25% dividend increase and a $1 billion buyback program, a testament to their improving fundamentals.

Solid Quarter To Close Out The Fiscal Year

Delta Air Lines reported their Q4 earnings to close out the year with mixed results. EPS was in line with analysts' estimates at $1.55, while revenue posted a solid beat by $310 million.

This amounted to $16 billion, a growth rate of 11.1% year-over-year. For the full year, revenue increased 2.3% from $57 billion to $58.3 billion, solid considering the tough economic backdrop. Top-line growth was a result of premium revenue increasing 7% and cargo revenue growing 9%.

DAL's performance should be commended due to volatile fuel prices, financially-constrained consumers as a result of rises in unemployment, historically high interest rates, and residual impacts from the government shutdown.

You can see from the chart below, Delta's revenue per seat available and per passenger declined slightly from the year prior. And yet total revenue increased.

Earnings per share declined year-over-year, down 5.5% from $6.16 in 2024 to $5.82. This was a result of lower pre-tax profits, which fell year-over-year from $1.6 billion to $1.3 billion.

Operating margins also fell from 12% to 10%. And while I expect near-term volatility for DAL, the company's fundamental improvements set the company up for solid growth in 2026 and beyond.

2026 Guidance

During the quarter, management issued guidance for 2026, with EPS expected in a range of $6.50-$7.50. At midpoint, this represents a growth rate of 20%, a complete turnaround from FY25.

Free cash flow is expected in a range of $3 billion to $4 billion, lower than 2025 due to increased CAPEX of $5.5 billion. Leverage is expected to continue trending downward, all positive signs going forward.

With interest rates expected to continue falling and a likelihood of seeing cost reductions, I anticipate management raising guidance as the quarters go on, resulting in a solid growth setup for 2026.

Growth Drivers

As previously mentioned, lower base rates should provide relief for DAL and its peers. Moreover, CPI cooled slightly ahead of expectations, showing that inflation seems to be under control despite tariffs.

The airline giant also recently announced a multi-year partnership with Sphere Entertainment (SPHR) to allow guests access to the Delta SKY360 Club lounge during events.

This makes DAL the official airline partner for SPHR. And as a result, DAL will receive promotion during events.

This should result in solid promotion and growth going forward, particularly with its rewards members.

They have also been leveraging artificial intelligence with the launch of Delta Concierge, an AI-powered assistant in the Delta app to assist and deliver support to airline members.

And lastly, the company continues to expand its fleet and opened a new Delta Sky Club, their second largest, in Salt Lake City, Utah.

Why DAL Could Become A Solid Dividend Grower

Although airlines are highly cyclical and considered riskier, Delta's fundamental setup appears poised to deliver solid dividend growth. As mentioned earlier, the company announced a $1 billion share buyback program in Q1.

For the fiscal year, management didn't repurchase any shares as they continued to prioritize paying down debt, a better option in my opinion. The current buyback program is for 3 years, and I expect management to opportunistically buy back shares during this time frame.

As a result of this and their attractive forward P/E, I expect management at DAL could see solid earnings growth over the next 3 to 5 years.

DAL re-initiated their dividend in 2023 and last raised it a solid 25%. And their dividend was well-covered with only $440 million in dividends paid for the entire year.

DAL also saw record free cash flow for the year with $4.6 billion, surpassing guidance set in Q4 of last year. Peer United Airlines (UAL) expects to generate $3 billion in FCF this fiscal year.

Management expected FCF in a range of $4 billion. This gives DAL a safe payout of just 9.6% and ample room for double-digit dividend growth going forward.

Moreover, I suspect solid, double-digit increases in the foreseeable future, which should drive share price growth as a result.

Improved Balance Sheet

Delta Air Lines also improved their balance sheet, managing to pay down debt and decrease leverage to 2.4x. This was 2.6x in Q1. And as previously mentioned, management is targeting 2.0x by the end of 2026 and a long-term target of 1.0x.

This was higher than peer Southwest Airlines (LUV), whose leverage ratio in their latest quarter was 2.1x.

Delta's liquidity was robust, with $7.4 billion, including $3.1 billion in revolver capacity. Net debt declined $3.7 billion from the prior year to $14 billion.

Total debt stood at $22 billion. Their strong cash flow generation also gives the company flexibility to continue deleveraging.

Attractive Valuation At Less Than 10x Earnings

At the current price, DAL's forward P/E sits at 9.81x, relatively low for a company of their caliber. Although airline stocks are considered riskier and highly cyclical, I believe the stock deserves to trade higher.

Using United Airlines' guidance midpoint of $10, they currently have a slightly higher P/E of 11.4x. Over the next 2 to 4 years, I suspect DAL can at least trade in the 12x-14x range despite this being above their 5-year average of 9.45x.

Because of COVID and the high inflationary environment over the past 5 or so years, I don't think DAL's 5-year average should be viewed as a reliable benchmark.

Furthermore, buybacks and partnerships could fuel growth, resulting in solid price appreciation. Additionally, lower interest rates should provide tailwinds. And together, I see solid upside over the long-term despite near- to medium-term volatility. Using approximately a 13x multiple, DAL offers strong upside over the next two years of 57%.

Risks & Conclusion

Despite CPI cooling, which should provide relief going forward, labor market uncertainty and geopolitical issues still pose risks. Moreover, airline stocks are highly cyclical, likely the reason they trade at low multiples.

If we see further labor market weakening, perhaps due to AI impacts, I expect DAL could continue seeing declining revenue per passenger and seat impact margins.

However, long-term, I like where Delta Air Lines is headed, as their improved balance sheet, robust cash flows, and a seemingly improving economy set them up for success in 2026 and beyond.

As a result, I maintain my Buy rating on DAL. Their well-covered dividend and low payout ratio could result in strong price appreciation for the foreseeable future.

data.title: Frontline: How Fleet Renewal, Venezuela Oil, And A 17% Yield Could Drive Returns (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
I have covered Frontline (FRO) before, where I outlined the company’s background in detail, explained why I considered it overvalued, and why I preferred Okeanis (ECO). Since the article was published, Frontline has returned 19%, while Okeanis has returned 47% over the same period. However, due to recent developments, I’m upgrading the stock from sell to buy. While I still prefer Okeanis, Frontline should benefit from the sector sentiment.

Background

Frontline is the largest tanker pure-play, and after a recent transaction, its future fleet will consist of 81 vessels: 42 VLCCs, 21 Suezmax tankers, and 18 LR2/Aframax tankers.

This transaction is transformative as the company will sell its eight oldest VLCCs, which have an average age of around 10 years, and acquire nine newbuilds, most of them delivering in 2026, for just $32 million per vessel.

Moreover, back in October there were rumors that Frontline was planning to sell its LR2 fleet. While the sale didn’t materialize, those rumors have resurfaced recently, suggesting that the company is still trying to sell those vessels.

This will transform the company into the largest and with the newest fleet among peers, especially if LR2s are finally sold.

Another reason for optimism is the return of Venezuelan oil to the legitimate market. Until now, most of Venezuelan oil was exported using dark fleet tankers headed to China; however, from now on, as the US will control the oil, it will be transported using legitimate tankers. This is highly relevant, as this is not just the reopening of a new route but the revival of a completely new market that was lost years ago. It represents the creation of new ton-miles for the legitimate fleet.

Frontline’s CEO, Lars Barstad, summarized it really well in a tweet:

Moreover, Venezuelan oil requires diluents for transportation. Until now, these were sourced from Russia or Iran in their dark fleet. However, now those tankers will no longer be used. Instead, diluents will come from the US with compliant tankers, and Vitol has already sent the first one.

As the tweet states, another potentially even bigger catalyst could be if Iranian oil returns to the legitimate fleet; however, despite the recent unrest, I’m not optimistic the Iranian regime could be overthrown.

Frontline is the best-positioned tanker stock to benefit from those developments, as it is the most liquid and the go-to stock when such events unfold, as it is the most known among retail and fund investors.

This favorable treatment is in part due to its generous dividend policy of paying quarterly dividends equal to or close to EPS, which translates into strong dividends and yields when rates soar.

Financial Position and Valuation

Frontline has a comfortable financial position with $189 million in cash and $3.2 billion in debt. After the announced fleet renewal transaction, the company will receive about $830 million during this quarter and will spend $1.2 billion on the new vessels; most of it will be paid towards delivery of each vessel from Q3 2026. This means that the debt will increase; however, the company will have one more ship and a much younger fleet.

While the company provided attractive guidance, it should be noted that this is based on load to discharge, which overstates the numbers. Despite this, and considering the rates evolution, Q4 will be the best quarter in the company’s recent history, as I expect an EPS of around $1.15 and a similar dividend. This implies a yield above 17%. Moreover, after a brief collapse in rates at year end, rates continue to be really strong, which ensures another great quarter in Q1.

Regarding the valuation, the data points from the transaction suggest that the company is not trading as expensively as it could seem, due to vessel prices rising quickly:

However, asset prices are at historical levels, and to be sustainable, rates have to remain strong on average. For the stock to continue to rise, it needs positive sentiment in the sector and good rates to start trading in relation to its yield more than its NAV.

I believe that the company could reach $30 per share (around 1.4x NAV) if momentum continues.

Risks

The elevated valuation carries risks, as huge expectations are priced in. If those expectations do not materialize, the stock will suffer.

Starting a position at current levels, beyond a trade while momentum supports it, requires elevated rates (not necessarily at current levels, but higher than historical levels). This is especially true when rates are entering the softest months of the year (Q2 and especially Q3).

Asset prices should remain elevated too, which requires high rates to justify current valuations.

Another important risk, related to the above, is if OPEC+ decides to slash output again. In that case, tanker demand will be reduced, and tanker rates will follow. For me, this is the most important risk.

Finally, a short-term risk is that the company will sell those vessels in Q1 and won’t receive any until Q3; this will substantially reduce the dividend capacity in Q2 and Q3.

Conclusion

Frontline has announced a transformative fleet renewal that will position the company as the largest and youngest public tanker fleet owner.

This, combined with positive momentum from the Venezuela news, leads me to upgrade the stock to a buy. However, to justify higher valuations, rates must continue to remain elevated in the future, and the stock should start trading more based on its yield rather than asset values.

data.title: Urgent Warning: These REITs Face High Risk Of Dividend Cuts
data.description:
Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) recently cut its dividend, and unfortunately for us, we did not see that one coming.

It was, of course, well-known that the life science property sector was oversupplied, but we thought that the REIT's long lease terms would limit the damage of this cyclical downturn. The REIT also had one of the strongest balance sheets in the entire REIT sector and a low payout ratio based on FFO.

But then the new administration came in and caused unprecedented policy chaos, which essentially killed the funding of this sector. No one is going to invest billions into the research and development of new drugs if the potential payoff on such investments is highly uncertain due to the constant threats of tariffs, pricing restrictions, new stricter regulations, and more.

As a result, the demand for these properties crashed, even as the sector was already oversupplied, making an already bad situation a lot worse, pushing ARE to cut its dividend in an abundance of caution.

This caught us off guard, and we paid the price:

This is a good example of what typically happens as a REIT cuts its dividend. Income investors and dividend-oriented funds indiscriminately sell the stock based on the change in capital allocation policy, regardless of the rest of the story.

The new imbalance in supply and demand for the stock then causes it to crash to much lower levels.

That is why it is generally preferable to avoid holding REITs that are at high risk of cutting their dividend.

Fortunately, we have been quite successful at that over the years, predicting the dividend cuts of many REITs, including:

  • Global Net Lease (GNL)
  • Easterly Government Properties (DEA)
  • Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT)
  • Service Properties Trust (SVC)
  • And many others...

Today, we are going to give you our latest dividend cut predictions.

Be very cautious about these three REITs:

KKR Real Estate Finance (KREF)

KKR Real Estate Finance (KREF) is a popular mortgage REIT.

It is popular because it is offering a 12.7% dividend yield, which many seem to think is sustainable, and the REIT is managed by the private equity powerhouse KKR (KKR).

The REIT already cut its dividend significantly in early 2024, and this appears to have led many to believe that the current dividend payout should now be sustainable. The management's own commentary on the earnings call also seems rather optimistic. They said this on the Q3 call:

"We set our dividend at a level in which we believe we can cover distributable earnings prior to realized losses over the long term."

An analyst even asked them about potentially hiking the dividend, which makes it seem as if the market is still sleeping on the risk of another cut.

But I think that investors should be concerned.

The current dividend is not covered by distributable cash flow, and the REIT is heavily leveraged with a debt-to-equity of about 4x.

The management remains optimistic because they think that they can sell some of their repossessed properties and reinvest the proceeds into new loans, which will then lead to a surge in distributable cash flow.

I hope for them that it works out well. But when you are so heavily leveraged, I think that everything needs to be executed to perfection for the dividend to be maintained.

And the big elephant in the room here is that the REIT's loan portfolio is today 14% invested in the life science sector. As we discussed in the introduction of this article, Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) is currently facing severe headwinds, and those same headwinds also apply to KREF.

In fact, KREF may face even greater headwinds given that its life science collateral is inferior in quality relative to the portfolio of Alexandria Real Estate on average.

In the last quarter, KREF had to downgrade the risk rating of a life science property, and I expect more of that in 2026.

The issue is that when you have so much leverage, each loss will result in a big step back, and this is what I think will ultimately lead to another dividend cut.

The main risk to the thesis is that KREF manages to rapidly sell some of its recently foreclosed assets and redeploys the proceeds accretively, leading to a recovery in dividend coverage. I think that this is unlikely.

One Liberty Properties (OLP)

One Liberty Properties (OLP) is a diversified net lease REIT focusing primarily on industrial properties.

A lot of investors hear "industrial" and immediately think "steady high growth" supported by e-commerce and onshoring.

But the industrial property sector is very versatile, and there exist vast differences in risk profiles and growth prospects.

A newly built e-commerce distribution center leased to Amazon (AMZN) may sell at a low 4% cap rate, even as an older manufacturing facility leased to a middle-market non-credit tenant may sell at a 9% cap rate.

Both belong to the industrial peer group, but their future prospects are day and night.

OLP is more heavily focused on those riskier industrial properties that sell at high cap rates.

That on its own does not mean that the dividend isn't sustainable.

But the problem is that these are clearly riskier, more cyclical assets (as you wouldn't get such high cap rates in the industrial property sector otherwise), and we may be approaching the end of the cycle.

We had nearly as many job layoffs in 2025 as in 2009, and the Fed is busy cutting rates because of this reason.

Even that would be fine if the REIT had a strong balance sheet and a low dividend payout ratio, but it is the opposite here.

The REIT has a high 6.4x Debt-to-EBITDA. Here is how its leverage compares to some of its net lease peers.

Debt-to-EBITDA
Agree Realty (ADC) 3.8x
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) 3.8x
One Liberty Properties (OLP) 6.4x

And its dividend payout ratio is also unusually high:

Payout Ratio
Agree Realty (ADC) 73%
Essential Properties Realty Trust (EPRT) 62%
One Liberty Properties (OLP) 100%

The REIT only has 4.5 years left on its leases on average, and I predict that as more leases reach expiration, the REIT will deal with growing vacancies, rising capex, and releasing costs, which will ultimately lead to a dividend cut.

The main risk to OLP's dividend cut thesis is if interest rates rapidly return to lower levels, reducing the balance sheet stress, even as the economy remains stronger than expected, resulting in better than expected releasing prospects.

National Storage Affiliates (NSA)

National Storage Affiliates (NSA) is the smallest of the self-storage REITs. Its close peers include CubeSmart (CUBE), Public Storage (PSA), and ExtraSpace Storage (EXR).

When its cost of capital was low, NSA was able to grow the fastest of its peer group, as its small size and the large investment spreads resulted in stronger accretion from new acquisitions.

This also resulted in stronger total returns for most of its history, which made it very popular among many REIT investors.

But then self-storage became oversupplied, and since then, NSA has been the worst performer in its peer group due to its smaller size, higher leverage, and weaker assets on average.

Its share price has dropped much more than that of its peers, and as a result, it now trades at a historically high 7.3% dividend. The spread relative to its peers is much larger than usual:

And given that the REIT's AFFO payout ratio is already above 100% and the REIT has high leverage with a 7x Debt-to-EBITDA, I think that a cut is likely just around the corner. Possibly already over the coming quarters.

The main risk to this thesis is a faster-than-expected recovery of the self-storage sector, resulting in strong same-property NOI growth already in the near term. This could potentially save them from a dividend cut.

I am actually bullish on the long-term prospects of the self-storage property sector, and we hold a position in CubeSmart at High-Yield Landlord but would not dare to invest in NSA due to the high risk of a dividend cut.

It is by staying away from such situations that we have managed to outperform the market over the long run.
